questions.q: In which Indian city was the first public film screening, held by agents of the Lumière brothers in 1896, organised?
questions.explanation: The first public film screening in India was held in Bombay (now Mumbai) by agents of the Lumière brothers in 1896.

questions.q: On what date in 1896 was the first public Lumière film screening held in Bombay?
questions.explanation: The first public film screening in India took place on 7 July 1896 in Bombay.

questions.q: At which Bombay venue did the Lumière brothers' agents hold India's first public film screening in 1896?
questions.explanation: The first public film screening in India was held at Watson's Hotel in Bombay on 7 July 1896.

questions.q: Whose travelling agents brought motion pictures to Bombay for the first public film screening in India in 1896?
questions.explanation: Agents of the Lumière brothers brought their cinematograph films to Bombay for India's first public screening.

questions.q: What was the name of the camera-projector device, invented by the Lumière brothers, used at the 1896 Bombay screening?
questions.explanation: The Lumière brothers' device, the Cinématographe (cinematograph), was used to project the films at the 1896 Bombay screening.

questions.q: Which famous short, often called 'Arrival of a Train', was among the films shown at the 1896 Lumière screening in Bombay?
questions.explanation: 'Arrival of a Train' was one of the Lumière shorts screened at Watson's Hotel in Bombay in 1896.

questions.q: The 1896 Bombay screening of Lumière films marked the arrival of what new medium in India?
questions.explanation: The 1896 Lumière screening in Bombay introduced cinema, the moving image, to Indian audiences.

questions.q: In which decade did the Lumière brothers' agents hold the first public film screening in India?
questions.explanation: The first public film screening in India was held in 1896, in the 1890s.

questions.q: The films shown at the 1896 Lumière Bombay screening were of what kind?
questions.explanation: The Lumière films shown in Bombay in 1896 were short silent black-and-white motion pictures.

questions.q: Roughly how long after the Lumières' first Paris screening did the 1896 Bombay screening take place?
questions.explanation: The Bombay screening came roughly six months after the Lumières' landmark first public Paris screening of December 1895.

questions.q: In which city did the Lumière brothers hold their celebrated first public film screening, which preceded the Bombay event by about six months?
questions.explanation: The Lumière brothers held their famous first paying public screening in Paris in December 1895, about six months before Bombay.

questions.q: What were the first names of the two Lumière brothers whose cinematograph films were screened in Bombay in 1896?
questions.explanation: The Lumière brothers were Louis and Auguste Lumière, pioneers of the cinematograph.

questions.q: In which country did the Lumière brothers, whose films reached Bombay in 1896, develop the cinematograph?
questions.explanation: The Lumière brothers were French, and the cinematograph was developed in France.

questions.q: Watson's Hotel, the site of the 1896 Lumière screening, is notable as one of Bombay's earliest buildings made largely of what material?
questions.explanation: Watson's Hotel in Bombay was an early prefabricated cast-iron building, among the oldest of its kind in India.

questions.q: Which Indian pioneer is generally credited with shooting the country's first films in the years after the 1896 Lumière screening, with shorts such as 'The Wrestlers'?
questions.explanation: Harishchandra Sakharam Bhatavdekar (Save Dada) shot some of India's earliest films, including 'The Wrestlers', shortly after the Lumière screening inspired him.

questions.q: The Lumière films shown in Bombay in 1896, like the brothers' other early shorts, were characterised by what subject matter?
options: Scenes of everyday life and real events (actualités)
Filmed stage plays with actors reciting dialogue
questions.explanation: The Lumière brothers' early films, including those shown in Bombay, were 'actualités' — short documentary-style scenes of everyday life and real events, such as a train arriving or workers leaving a factory.

questions.q: The 1896 Lumière screening in Bombay is considered the starting point of what in India?
questions.explanation: The 1896 Bombay screening is regarded as the beginning of film exhibition and the cinema-going experience in India.

questions.q: How many short films were typically shown in the Lumière programme presented to Bombay audiences in 1896?
options: A programme of several short films
questions.explanation: The Lumière programme consisted of a series of short films, each running only a minute or so, rather than a single long picture.

questions.q: After the initial Watson's Hotel shows in 1896, the Lumière screenings in Bombay moved to which type of larger venue?
questions.explanation: Following the first shows at Watson's Hotel, the Lumière screenings shifted to the larger Novelty Theatre in Bombay.

questions.q: Which newspaper of the period famously advertised the 1896 Bombay Lumière shows as 'the marvel of the century'?
questions.explanation: The Times of India carried advertisements for the Lumière cinematograph shows in Bombay in 1896.

questions.q: The 1896 Lumière screening in Bombay predated India's first full-length feature film, Raja Harishchandra, by roughly how many years?
questions.explanation: Raja Harishchandra was released in 1913, about seventeen years after the 1896 Lumière screening.

questions.q: What technical feature distinguished the Lumière cinematograph used in Bombay from Edison's earlier Kinetoscope?
options: It was viewed by one person through a peephole
It projected images onto a screen for an audience
questions.explanation: Unlike Edison's peephole Kinetoscope, the Lumière cinematograph projected films onto a screen so a whole audience could watch together.

questions.q: Which Lumière operator is generally identified as the agent who presented the cinematograph films in Bombay in 1896?
questions.explanation: Marius Sestier is the Lumière agent generally credited with presenting the cinematograph in Bombay in 1896 on his way to Australia.

questions.q: Besides 'Arrival of a Train', which other famous Lumière short was part of the films shown to early Bombay audiences in 1896?
options: Leaving the Factory (Workers Leaving the Lumière Factory)
questions.explanation: Classic Lumière shorts such as 'Workers Leaving the Lumière Factory' featured in the early programmes shown in Bombay.

questions.q: In December 1895, before the Bombay screening, the Lumière brothers held their first paying public screening at which Paris location?
options: The Salon Indien of the Grand Café
questions.explanation: The Lumières' first paying public screening took place at the Salon Indien of the Grand Café in Paris in December 1895.

questions.q: Watson's Hotel, the venue of the 1896 Lumière screening, was located in which area of Bombay?
questions.explanation: Watson's Hotel stood in the Esplanade / Kala Ghoda area of the Fort district of Bombay.

questions.q: By what alternative name is Watson's Hotel, the site of the 1896 Lumière screening, also known today?
questions.explanation: The old Watson's Hotel building is now known as Esplanade Mansion.

questions.q: The Lumière brothers' family business, before they turned to cinema, was primarily manufacturing what product?
questions.explanation: The Lumière family ran a successful business manufacturing photographic plates and film before developing the cinematograph.

questions.q: Reflecting attitudes of the colonial era, the very first Watson's Hotel screenings in 1896 were attended chiefly by which audience?
options: A largely European and elite clientele
questions.explanation: Watson's Hotel catered to a largely European and elite clientele, so the first 1896 screenings there drew chiefly that audience.

questions.q: The arrival of the Lumière cinematograph in Bombay in 1896 occurred during which period of Indian history?
questions.explanation: The 1896 Bombay screening took place during the era of British colonial rule in India, decades before independence in 1947.
